senses.definition: plural of sourdine, a mute or device that dampens sound, especially for a musical instrument or firearm.
examples: The orchestra used sourdines to quiet the brass section.
The hunter attached sourdines to his rifle to reduce recoil and noise.

senses.definition: a hard, sour-smelling fruit stone or a variant of a common name; not widely attested in standard dictionaries.
examples: The old herbarium notes mention a fruit called sourdock, though specimens are rare.
Local dialects sometimes refer to certain wild plants as sourdock.

senses.definition: past tense of sourdo, a nonstandard or dialectal verb form; not widely attested in standard English.
examples: In the old tale, the hero sourdook a rival to stand still.
Some dialect dictionaries list sourdook as a variant of cook or sour-verb forms.

senses.definition: a dough from previous baking that contains natural yeasts; used to start a new batch of bread.
examples: The baker saved a bit of sourdough to make the next loaf rise well.
Sourdough starters require regular feeding with flour and water.

senses.definition: a person who bakes or eats sourdough bread; informal and historical usage.
examples: The sourdough shared tales of long journeys during the gold rush.
Old-time bakers were proud to be known as sourdoughs.

senses.definition: plural of sourdough in the sense of bread bakers or starters.
examples: The sourdoughs of the village swapped starters before winter.
Two sourdoughs from San Francisco came to teach the class.

senses.definition: a dialect or misspelling; not established in standard English.
examples: The manuscript shows a scribal error 'sourdre' for 'sourdough'.
In dialect notes, sourdre appears as a regional term.

senses.definition: tasted or smelling sour; spoiled or turned sour in flavor.
examples: The milk has soured and should be discarded.
Her mood soured after hearing the bad news.

senses.definition: to become sour or cause to become sour; to spoil.
examples: Milk will sour if left out too long.
A bad decision can sour the entire project.

senses.definition: the state or quality of being sour; sour flavor or mood.
examples: The souredness of the milk indicated it was unsafe to drink.
Her souredness grew after the argument.

senses.definition: archaic or dialect term; not commonly used in modern standard English.
examples: The glossary notes souren as a regional plant name.

senses.definition: more sour in taste or mood; comparative form of sour.
examples: Lemon juice is sourer than orange juice.
Her sourer mood after the drought lasted days.

senses.definition: most sour in taste or mood; superlative form of sour.
examples: The sourest lemon is still not as sour as his attitude.
They faced the sourest critique of the day.

senses.definition: a plant of a sour-gum family; name used for certain trees or gums in dialects.
examples: Indigenous peoples used sourgum for medicinal purposes.
The sourgum tree exudes a strong resin.

senses.definition: plural of sourgum; trees or resins of the sourgum group.
examples: The forest contained several sourgums used for timber.
Medicinal salves were made from sourgums.

senses.definition: having a sour or unkind disposition; ill-tempered.
examples: The sourhearted landlord was hard to please.
Her sourhearted reply dampened the mood.

senses.definition: the act of becoming sour; the process of souring.
examples: Souring of milk happens when it stays warm too long.
The relationship is souring due to constant disputes.

senses.definition: plural of souring; instances of souring processes or events.
examples: Sourings of dairy products indicate poor storage.
There were several sourings in the negotiations.

senses.definition: having a sourish taste; somewhat sour.
examples: The sourish taste of the yogurt surprised me.
A sourish note hung in the air after the argument.

senses.definition: in a sour or sour-faced manner; with bitterness.
examples: He smiled sourishly and walked away.
She spoke sourishly, blaming everyone but herself.

senses.definition: the quality of being sour or tart in taste or mood.
examples: The sourishness of the lemonade lingered after the last sip.
Her sourishness masked her nervousness.

senses.definition: a term with limited usage; may refer to a variety of sour-tasting fruit or a nickname; not widely standard.
examples: locals used the nickname sourjack for a tart apple variety.

senses.definition: young or small sour creature or plant; dialectal usage.
examples: A sourling fled the market stall.
The gardener tended the sourlings first.

senses.definition: in a sour manner or with a sour expression; unfriendly.
examples: He answered sourly when questioned about the delay.
She looked sourly at the noisy crowd.

senses.definition: the quality of being sour; tart taste or bitter mood.
examples: The sourness of the lemonade made my face pucker.
There was a note of sourness in his voice.

senses.definition: plural of sourness; multiple instances of sour taste or mood.
examples: The sournesses of the citrus fruits varied.
They documented the sournesses in the tasting notes.

senses.definition: dialectal or place name; not widely recognized in standard English.
examples: The map marks Sourock as a small coastal hamlet.
